summary:
The commonest cause of temporomandibular joint ankylosis appears to be traumatic injury due to its widespread occurrence after such incidents and consistent mention across various studies highlighting this association over infectious processes specifically linked with TMJ ankyloses. Agent1 reasoned that although infection can lead to TMJ issues like septic arthritis, it was not mentioned prominently enough relative to trauma which has been consistently associated more strongly based on incidence data from multiple sources focusing primarily on traumas affecting the jaw area resulting in long-term complications including ankylosis. 

The summary captures two main aspects highlighted by Agent1’s detailed analysis – first, how trauma leads to increased instances of TMJ problems because of physical impacts often seen clinically; secondly, why specific types of infections aren't considered top causes despite being significant contributors elsewhere since their role isn’t emphasized nearly as much when discussing initial formation or predominant reasons behind permanent fixation conditions within joints generally referred to under 'TMJ Ankylosis'. This aligns closely with original content but condenses into concise statements addressing core elements without extraneous details.
